terms.definition: A system architecture where multiple autonomous AI models, or 'agents,' are assigned specialized roles and work collaboratively to solve complex problems. These frameworks use a coordination layer to manage communication and task delegation, allowing for higher precision and cross-verification in sensitive fields like oncology.
terms.analogy: In a high-end kitchen, this is the 'Brigade de Cuisine' system: rather than one chef attempting to cook every dish, you have a specialized saucier, a pastry chef, and a grill master all working in concert to deliver a single, perfect five-course meal.
terms.business_value: Shifts AI from a general-purpose chatbot to a reliable autonomous workforce. This enables the automation of high-stakes, multi-step professional workflows that previously required a team of human subject matter experts.

terms.definition: The capability of an LLM to perform deductive, symbolic logic to solve novel problems rather than relying solely on pattern recognition from its training data. This represents a transition from 'probabilistic guessing' to 'verifiable discovery,' allowing models to optimize mathematical bounds and prove original theorems without human guidance.
terms.analogy: If traditional AI is a GPS that follows pre-mapped roads, AMR is an off-road vehicle that can navigate uncharted territory by calculating the physics of the terrain and building its own path as it moves forward.
terms.business_value: When AI moves from 'summarizing the past' to 'inventing the future' through original PhD-level research, it accelerates R&D cycles in cryptography, material science, and pharmaceutical drug discovery at a fraction of the current cost.

terms.definition: The design and production of Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs) optimized exclusively for neural network training and inference. Unlike general-purpose GPUs, these chips require massive capital expenditure (CapEx) and guaranteed volume commitments from cloud providers to offset the multi-billion dollar costs of fabrication.
terms.analogy: Imagine commissioning a custom-built race car engine tailored for one specific track; the manufacturer won't even start the factory line unless your business partner signs a contract to pay for 40% of the fuel and maintenance for the next five years.
terms.business_value: This reflects the 'CapEx Moat' in the AI industry. Success is no longer just about who has the best algorithm, but who has the balance sheet and ecosystem partnerships to control their own hardware supply chain and reduce long-term compute costs.
